title: Create and inspect a Pod
description: Create a namespace `basics`. In that namespace, create a Pod named `nginx` using the image `nginx:stable` with the label `app=web`. Verify the pod is Running.
hints: Use `kubectl run nginx --image=nginx:stable --labels=app=web -n basics`
Check pod status with `kubectl get pod nginx -n basics`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace basics --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod nginx -n basics -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'nginx' is not Running in namespace 'basics'

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod nginx -n basics -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels.app}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'nginx' missing label app=web

title: Create and scale a Deployment
description: Create a Deployment named `web` in namespace `basics` with image `nginx:stable` and 1 replica. Then scale it to 3 replicas.
hints: Use `kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:stable -n basics`
Scale with `kubectl scale deployment web --replicas=3 -n basics`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace basics --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get deployment web -n basics -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'
validation_checks.message: Deployment 'web' does not have 3 replicas

validation_checks.command: kubectl get deployment web -n basics -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}'
validation_checks.message: Deployment 'web' does not have 3 ready replicas

title: Expose a Deployment as a ClusterIP Service
description: In namespace `basics`, expose the Deployment `web` as a ClusterIP Service named `web-svc` on port 80, targeting container port 80.
hints: Use `kubectl expose deployment web --name=web-svc --port=80 --target-port=80 -n basics`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace basics --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:stable -n basics --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get svc web-svc -n basics -o jsonpath='{.spec.type}'
validation_checks.message: Service 'web-svc' not found or wrong type

validation_checks.command: kubectl get svc web-svc -n basics -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[0].port}'
validation_checks.message: Service 'web-svc' port is not 80

title: ConfigMap as environment variables
description: In namespace `basics`, create a ConfigMap named `app-config` with key `APP_ENV=production`. Create a Pod named `app` using image `busybox:stable` that reads this ConfigMap and sets the environment variable `APP_ENV` from it. The pod should run `sleep 3600`.
hints: Use `kubectl create configmap app-config --from-literal=APP_ENV=production -n basics`
In the pod spec use `envFrom` or `env[].valueFrom.configMapKeyRef`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace basics --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get configmap app-config -n basics -o jsonpath='{.data.APP_ENV}'
validation_checks.message: ConfigMap 'app-config' missing key APP_ENV=production

validation_checks.command: kubectl exec -n basics app -- env 2>/dev/null | grep APP_ENV
validation_checks.message: Pod 'app' does not have APP_ENV=production in its environment

title: RBAC: Role and RoleBinding
description:
Create a namespace `rbac-test`. In that namespace:
1. Create a ServiceAccount named `app-sa`.
2. Create a Role named `pod-reader` that allows `get`, `list`, and `watch` on `pods`.
3. Create a RoleBinding named `app-sa-pod-reader` that binds `pod-reader` to `app-sa`.
hints: Use `kubectl create serviceaccount`, `kubectl create role`, and `kubectl create rolebinding` for speed.
RoleBinding subject kind is `ServiceAccount`.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace rbac-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get serviceaccount app-sa -n rbac-test -o name
validation_checks.message: ServiceAccount 'app-sa' not found in rbac-test

validation_checks.command: kubectl get role pod-reader -n rbac-test -o jsonpath='{.rules[0].verbs}'
validation_checks.message: Role 'pod-reader' missing expected verbs (get, list, watch)

validation_checks.command: kubectl get rolebinding app-sa-pod-reader -n rbac-test -o jsonpath='{.subjects[0].name}'
validation_checks.message: RoleBinding 'app-sa-pod-reader' not bound to app-sa

title: Cordon and drain a node
description: Cordon the node `aks-lab-m02` so no new pods are scheduled on it, then safely drain it (ignoring DaemonSets, deleting emptyDir data).
hints: `kubectl cordon <node>` marks it unschedulable.
`kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data`

validation_checks.command: kubectl get node aks-lab-m02 -o jsonpath='{.spec.unschedulable}'
validation_checks.message: Node 'aks-lab-m02' is not cordoned (spec.unschedulable != true)

title: PersistentVolume and PersistentVolumeClaim
description: Create a namespace `storage-test`. Create a PersistentVolume named `lab-pv` with 1Gi capacity, `ReadWriteOnce` access mode, `Retain` reclaim policy, and `hostPath` at `/mnt/lab-pv`. Then create a PersistentVolumeClaim named `lab-pvc` in `storage-test` that binds to it requesting 500Mi.
hints: PV is cluster-scoped (no namespace). PVC is namespaced.
The PVC will bind to the PV because 500Mi <= 1Gi and access mode matches.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace storage-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pv lab-pv -o jsonpath='{.spec.capacity.storage}'
validation_checks.message: PersistentVolume 'lab-pv' not found or capacity is not 1Gi

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pvc lab-pvc -n storage-test -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
validation_checks.message: PVC 'lab-pvc' is not Bound

title: NetworkPolicy: default deny + allow specific traffic
description:
In namespace `netpol-test`:
1. Apply a default-deny NetworkPolicy named `default-deny` that denies all ingress traffic.
2. Apply a NetworkPolicy named `allow-web` that allows ingress on port 80 only from pods with label `role=frontend`.
hints: Default deny: `podSelector: {}` with an empty `ingress: []` array.
The allow policy uses `from.podSelector.matchLabels`.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace netpol-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get networkpolicy default-deny -n netpol-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.podSelector}'
validation_checks.message: NetworkPolicy 'default-deny' not found or does not select all pods

validation_checks.command: kubectl get networkpolicy allow-web -n netpol-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.ingress[0].ports[0].port}'
validation_checks.message: NetworkPolicy 'allow-web' does not allow port 80

title: etcd snapshot backup and restore
description: Take a snapshot of the etcd database and save it to `/tmp/etcd-snapshot.db`. Then verify the snapshot is valid.
hints: `ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save` — you need the cert flags.
Certs are usually in `/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/`. CA: `ca.crt`, cert: `server.crt`, key: `server.key`.
Verify with `etcdctl snapshot status /tmp/etcd-snapshot.db`

validation_checks.command: test -f /tmp/etcd-snapshot.db && echo ok
validation_checks.message: Snapshot file /tmp/etcd-snapshot.db does not exist

validation_checks.command: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /tmp/etcd-snapshot.db --write-out=simple 2>/dev/null | head -1
validation_checks.message: Snapshot file is not a valid etcd snapshot

title: kubeadm cluster upgrade sequence
description: When upgrading a Kubernetes cluster using kubeadm, what is the correct order of operations?

choices.text: Upgrade worker nodes first, then upgrade the control plane
Run `kubeadm upgrade apply` on the control plane, drain and upgrade each worker node, then uncordon
Upgrade kubelet on all nodes simultaneously, then run `kubeadm upgrade apply`
Drain all nodes first, then upgrade control plane, then upgrade workers

explanation: The correct sequence is: (1) upgrade kubeadm on the control plane node, (2) run `kubeadm upgrade plan` then `kubeadm upgrade apply`, (3) upgrade kubelet/kubectl on the control plane, (4) for each worker: drain → upgrade kubeadm/kubelet/kubectl → `kubeadm upgrade node` → uncordon.

title: Troubleshoot: CrashLoopBackOff pod
description:
A pod named `broken-app` in namespace `troubleshoot` is in CrashLoopBackOff. Investigate the root cause, fix it, and ensure the pod reaches Running state.

**Setup:** The pod is pre-deployed with a missing required environment variable. It expects `DATABASE_URL` to be set but it is not, causing the app to exit immediately.
hints: `kubectl logs broken-app -n troubleshoot --previous` shows the last crash output.
`kubectl describe pod broken-app -n troubleshoot` shows environment variables.
Patch the deployment or edit the pod spec to add the missing env var: `DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost/app`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace troubleshoot --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl run broken-app --image=busybox:stable -n troubleshoot --restart=Always -- sh -c 'echo Starting; [ -z "$DATABASE_URL" ] && echo ERROR: DATABASE_URL not set && exit 1; sleep 3600'

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod broken-app -n troubleshoot -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'broken-app' is not in Running state

title: Troubleshoot: Node NotReady — first steps
description: A node shows `NotReady` status. After `kubectl describe node <node>`, you see the `KubeletNotReady` condition with message `PLEG is not healthy`. What is the most likely cause and best first action?

choices.text: The container runtime is unresponsive — check and restart containerd: `systemctl restart containerd`
The API server is down — check `kube-apiserver` pod logs
The node has a network partition — check firewall rules
etcd is unreachable — run `etcdctl endpoint health`

explanation: PLEG (Pod Lifecycle Event Generator) depends on the container runtime. When PLEG is unhealthy it usually means containerd (or another runtime) has stopped responding. Restarting containerd is the correct first action. If the runtime is healthy but the kubelet itself is misconfigured, `systemctl restart kubelet` would be next.

title: Troubleshoot: CoreDNS resolution failure
description:
A pod in namespace `dns-test` named `dns-client` cannot resolve the Kubernetes service `kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local`. Investigate and fix the DNS resolution issue.

**Clue:** The CoreDNS ConfigMap has been tampered with — the forward rule points to an invalid upstream.
hints: `kubectl exec -n dns-test dns-client -- nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local` to confirm failure.
`kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml` to inspect the Corefile.
The forward line should point to `8.8.8.8` or the cluster's upstream, not a broken IP.
After editing the ConfigMap, restart CoreDNS: `kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace dns-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl run dns-client --image=busybox:stable -n dns-test -- sleep 3600

validation_checks.command: kubectl exec -n dns-test dns-client -- nslookup kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local 2>&1 | grep -i 'address\|Server'
validation_checks.message: DNS resolution of kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local still failing

title: etcd quorum and fault tolerance
description: A production etcd cluster has 5 members. What is the maximum number of member failures it can tolerate while maintaining quorum?
explanation: etcd requires a majority quorum: (n/2)+1. For 5 members, quorum is 3, so 2 failures can be tolerated before losing quorum.

title: Control plane certificate renewal
description: Which command checks the expiry date of all control plane certificates managed by kubeadm?

choices.text: `openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -noout -dates`
`kubeadm certs check-expiration`
`kubectl get secrets -n kube-system | grep tls`
`kubeadm token list`

explanation: `kubeadm certs check-expiration` lists all certificates managed by kubeadm with their expiry dates in one command. The openssl option works for a single cert but is slower for auditing all certs at once.

title: Rolling update and rollback
description: In namespace `rollout-test`, there is a Deployment named `webapp` running `nginx:1.24`. Update the image to `nginx:1.25`. Once the rollout completes, roll it back to the previous version.
hints: `kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.25 -n rollout-test`
`kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp -n rollout-test`
`kubectl rollout undo deployment/webapp -n rollout-test`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace rollout-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create deployment webapp --image=nginx:1.24 -n rollout-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get deployment webapp -n rollout-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
validation_checks.message: Deployment 'webapp' image is not rolled back to nginx:1.24

validation_checks.command: kubectl rollout history deployment/webapp -n rollout-test | wc -l
validation_checks.message: Rollout history does not show at least 2 revisions (update + rollback)

title: CronJob with concurrency policy
description: In namespace `cron-test`, create a CronJob named `cleanup` that runs every 5 minutes. It should use image `busybox:stable` and execute `echo cleanup done`. Set `concurrencyPolicy` to `Forbid` and keep only the last 3 successful job records.
hints: `kubectl create cronjob cleanup --image=busybox:stable --schedule='*/5 * * * *' -- echo 'cleanup done' -n cron-test`
Patch with `kubectl patch cronjob cleanup -n cron-test` or use `kubectl edit` to add `concurrencyPolicy` and `successfulJobsHistoryLimit`.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace cron-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get cronjob cleanup -n cron-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.schedule}'
validation_checks.message: CronJob 'cleanup' schedule is not '*/5 * * * *'

validation_checks.command: kubectl get cronjob cleanup -n cron-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.concurrencyPolicy}'
validation_checks.message: CronJob 'cleanup' concurrencyPolicy is not Forbid

validation_checks.command: kubectl get cronjob cleanup -n cron-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.successfulJobsHistoryLimit}'
validation_checks.message: CronJob 'cleanup' successfulJobsHistoryLimit is not 3

title: Resource requests and limits
description: In namespace `limits-test`, create a Pod named `resource-demo` using image `nginx:stable`. Set resource requests to `100m` CPU and `128Mi` memory, and limits to `250m` CPU and `256Mi` memory.
hints: Use `kubectl run resource-demo --image=nginx:stable -n limits-test --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml`, edit to add resources, then apply.
Or use `kubectl set resources` after creation.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace limits-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod resource-demo -n limits-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'resource-demo' CPU request is not 100m

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod resource-demo -n limits-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources.limits.memory}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'resource-demo' memory limit is not 256Mi

title: Liveness and readiness probes
description:
In namespace `probes-test`, create a Pod named `probed-app` using image `nginx:stable`. Configure:
- A liveness probe: HTTP GET on port 80 at `/`, initial delay 10s, period 15s.
- A readiness probe: HTTP GET on port 80 at `/`, initial delay 5s, period 10s.
hints: Use `kubectl run --dry-run=client -o yaml` to generate a pod template then edit.
Both probes use `httpGet` with `path` and `port`.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace probes-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod probed-app -n probes-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].livenessProbe.httpGet.path}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'probed-app' liveness probe httpGet path is not '/'

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod probed-app -n probes-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].readinessProbe.initialDelaySeconds}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'probed-app' readiness probe initialDelaySeconds is not 5

title: Pod log retrieval options
description: A pod named `api` has been restarting. You want to see the logs from its **previous** container run (the one that crashed), not the current run. Which command retrieves those logs?

choices.text: `kubectl logs api --all-containers`
`kubectl logs api --previous`
`kubectl logs api --since=1h`
`kubectl describe pod api | tail -50`

explanation: `kubectl logs --previous` (or `-p`) retrieves logs from the terminated/previous instance of the container. `--all-containers` streams all containers in the pod. `--since` filters by time on the current run.

title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
description: In namespace `hpa-test`, there is a Deployment named `web`. Create an HPA named `web-hpa` that scales `web` between 1 and 5 replicas, targeting 70% CPU utilisation.
hints: `kubectl autoscale deployment web --cpu-percent=70 --min=1 --max=5 -n hpa-test --name=web-hpa`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace hpa-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:stable -n hpa-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get hpa web-hpa -n hpa-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.maxReplicas}'
validation_checks.message: HPA 'web-hpa' maxReplicas is not 5

validation_checks.command: kubectl get hpa web-hpa -n hpa-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.minReplicas}'
validation_checks.message: HPA 'web-hpa' minReplicas is not 1

title: Pod Security Context: non-root, read-only root filesystem
description:
In namespace `security-test`, create a Pod named `secure-pod` using image `nginx:stable` with the following security hardening:
- Run as user ID `1000`
- Read-only root filesystem
- Disallow privilege escalation
- Drop ALL Linux capabilities
hints: Use `securityContext` at both pod and container level.
`readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` is a container-level setting.
`runAsUser` and `runAsNonRoot` are pod-level settings.
nginx requires write access to /tmp and /var/cache/nginx — add emptyDir volumes if needed.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace security-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod secure-pod -n security-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.securityContext.runAsUser}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'secure-pod' is not configured to run as user 1000

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod secure-pod -n security-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'secure-pod' does not have readOnlyRootFilesystem=true

validation_checks.command: kubectl get pod secure-pod -n security-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext.capabilities.drop[0]}'
validation_checks.message: Pod 'secure-pod' does not drop ALL capabilities

title: RBAC: Least-privilege ClusterRole
description: A ServiceAccount `monitor-sa` in namespace `least-priv` currently has a ClusterRoleBinding to `cluster-admin`. This is too permissive. Replace it with a ClusterRole named `monitor-role` that allows only `get` and `list` on `pods`, `nodes`, and `namespaces` (cluster-scoped). Bind `monitor-sa` to this new ClusterRole.
hints: Delete the existing ClusterRoleBinding first.
`kubectl create clusterrole monitor-role --verb=get,list --resource=pods,nodes,namespaces`
`kubectl create clusterrolebinding monitor-binding --clusterrole=monitor-role --serviceaccount=least-priv:monitor-sa`
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace least-priv --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create serviceaccount monitor-sa -n least-priv --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create clusterrolebinding monitor-admin --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=least-priv:monitor-sa --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get clusterrolebinding monitor-admin -o name 2>&1
validation_checks.message: The cluster-admin ClusterRoleBinding 'monitor-admin' still exists — it must be removed

validation_checks.command: kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:least-priv:monitor-sa --all-namespaces
validation_checks.message: ServiceAccount monitor-sa cannot list pods

validation_checks.command: kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=system:serviceaccount:least-priv:monitor-sa --all-namespaces
validation_checks.message: ServiceAccount monitor-sa can still delete pods — permissions are too broad

title: Pod Security Admission: enforce restricted profile
description: Create a namespace `psa-test` and label it to enforce the `restricted` Pod Security Standard at `latest` version. Then verify that a Pod attempting to run as root is rejected.
hints: Label: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted` and `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest`
Try: `kubectl run test-root --image=nginx:stable -n psa-test` — this should be rejected because nginx runs as root by default.

validation_checks.command: kubectl get namespace psa-test -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels.pod-security\.kubernetes\.io/enforce}'
validation_checks.message: Namespace 'psa-test' does not have pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted label

title: Supply chain: Trivy image vulnerability scan
description: Use Trivy to scan the image `nginx:1.21` for CRITICAL severity vulnerabilities. Save the output to `/tmp/trivy-report.txt`. Then identify the total count of CRITICAL vulnerabilities found.
hints: `trivy image --severity CRITICAL nginx:1.21 > /tmp/trivy-report.txt`
`grep -c CRITICAL /tmp/trivy-report.txt` gives a rough count.
Trivy is pre-installed in the exam-sim pod and the toolbox.

validation_checks.command: test -f /tmp/trivy-report.txt && echo ok
validation_checks.message: Trivy report not found at /tmp/trivy-report.txt

validation_checks.command: grep -c 'CRITICAL' /tmp/trivy-report.txt 2>/dev/null || echo 0
validation_checks.message: No CRITICAL vulnerabilities found — check if the scan ran correctly against nginx:1.21

title: Supply chain: Kyverno policy to block latest tag
description: Create a Kyverno ClusterPolicy named `disallow-latest-tag` that blocks any Pod using an image tagged `:latest` (or with no tag, which defaults to latest). Test it by attempting to create a pod with `nginx:latest` — it should be rejected.
hints: Use a `validate` rule with `pattern` matching on `spec.containers[].image`.
The pattern should use `!(*:latest)` or a regex to catch untagged images.
Kyverno is pre-installed in the lab.

validation_checks.command: kubectl get clusterpolicy disallow-latest-tag -o jsonpath='{.spec.rules[0].name}' 2>/dev/null
validation_checks.message: ClusterPolicy 'disallow-latest-tag' not found

validation_checks.command: kubectl run test-latest --image=nginx:latest --dry-run=server -n default 2>&1
validation_checks.message: Kyverno policy did not block a pod using nginx:latest

title: Falco: detect shell spawned in container
description: You want Falco to alert when a shell binary (`/bin/sh`, `/bin/bash`) is executed inside any running container. Which Falco rule condition correctly matches this behaviour?

choices.text: `evt.type=execve and container.id != host and proc.name in (sh, bash)`
`evt.type=open and fd.name contains /bin/sh`
`container.id != host and proc.cmdline contains shell`
`evt.type=connect and proc.name=bash`

explanation: Falco uses `evt.type=execve` to catch process execution events. `container.id != host` excludes the host namespace. `proc.name in (sh, bash)` matches the common shell binaries. Option B catches file opens (not execution). Option C and D use incorrect field/event combinations.

title: Kubernetes audit policy: log secret access
description: You need to configure an audit policy that logs all `get` and `list` requests for `secrets` at `RequestResponse` level (including the response body), while logging everything else at `Metadata` level only. Which policy structure achieves this?

choices.text: One rule for secrets at RequestResponse, one catch-all rule at Metadata — secrets rule must come first
One catch-all rule at RequestResponse, with secrets excluded using `omitStages`
Two rules at RequestResponse — one for secrets, one for everything else
One rule at Metadata with secrets overridden via a separate `secretsLevel` field

explanation: Kubernetes audit policy rules are evaluated in order and the first matching rule applies. The secrets rule at `RequestResponse` must appear before the catch-all `Metadata` rule, otherwise the catch-all matches first and secrets requests are only logged at Metadata level. There is no `secretsLevel` field — that is not a real API field.

title: Immutable Secret
description: In namespace `immutable-test`, create a Secret named `db-creds` with key `password=S3cr3t!` and mark it as immutable.
hints: `kubectl create secret generic db-creds --from-literal=password=S3cr3t! -n immutable-test --dry-run=client -o yaml`
Add `immutable: true` to the Secret manifest before applying.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace immutable-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get secret db-creds -n immutable-test -o jsonpath='{.immutable}'
validation_checks.message: Secret 'db-creds' is not marked as immutable

title: NetworkPolicy: protect kube-system metadata
description:
Create a NetworkPolicy named `block-metadata` in namespace `metadata-test` that prevents all pods in that namespace from reaching the cloud metadata API at `169.254.169.254` (port 80).

Note: Use an egress rule with `ipBlock` CIDR `169.254.169.254/32` and an `except` block, or a deny-all approach.
hints: Use an egress NetworkPolicy on all pods in the namespace.
Use `ipBlock.cidr: 0.0.0.0/0` with `except: [169.254.169.254/32]` to allow all egress except the metadata endpoint.
Or use a default-deny egress policy and explicitly allow only needed traffic.
setup_commands: kubectl create namespace metadata-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

validation_checks.command: kubectl get networkpolicy block-metadata -n metadata-test -o jsonpath='{.spec.egress}' 2>/dev/null
validation_checks.message: NetworkPolicy 'block-metadata' not found or has no egress rules
